title: Weaving monk's belt on four shafts
subtitle: How to thread, sley, and treadle a traditional two-colour monk's belt draft on a four-shaft loom
excerpt: Monk's belt is a traditional Scandinavian block pattern woven on a straight four-shaft threading with a tabby tie-up. Pattern weft floats alternate with tabby rows to build rectangular blocks of colour against a plain weave ground. It is one of the most accessible four-shaft pattern structures and a useful foundation for understanding float-based weave design.
sourceNotes: Monk's belt is documented in Scandinavian weaving tradition and in standard UK and US weave structure references. The four-shaft straight threading and tabby tie-up described are consistent across all sources.

glossaryTerms.definition: A traditional Scandinavian weave structure producing rectangular blocks of pattern weft floats against a plain weave ground. Woven on a straight four-shaft threading with two shuttles: one for tabby and one for pattern. The block size is controlled by the number of pattern weft passes per sequence.

A diagram or notation showing which shaft each warp thread is threaded through. The threading draft determines which threads lift together during weaving. A straight draft threads shafts 1-2-3-4 in sequence, repeating across the full warp width.

Plain weave: every alternate warp thread is raised, producing the simplest interlacement. In monk's belt, tabby rows are woven between each pattern row to lock the pattern floats in place and prevent them from sliding.

content.text: is one of the first four-shaft patterns most weavers attempt. The
is a simple straight progression (1-2-3-4, repeat). The treadling alternates between two operations: a pattern row and a
row. The block size is controlled by the number of consecutive pattern passes, so the design can be varied without re-threading the loom.

items.name: Warp and tabby weft yarn: 8/2 cotton or equivalent, colour A
Pattern weft yarn: heavier yarn or multiple ends, colour B
Four-shaft floor loom or table loom with tabby tie-up
Two shuttles, one for tabby and one for pattern
Warping board for measuring warp length

content.text: Thread the warp on a straight 1-2-3-4 sequence, repeating across the full width. For a 15 cm wide sampler at 10 ends per cm, thread 150 ends across all four shafts in the repeating sequence. Sley the reed at the same density as the threading. Tie up shafts 1 and 3 to one treadle and shafts 2 and 4 to a second treadle for the tabby pairs; tie additional treadles for the pattern blocks (shafts 1 and 2 together, and shafts 3 and 4 together) to create the two pattern block positions.

For each block sequence: treadle pattern block A (shafts 1 and 2), pass the pattern weft shuttle; treadle tabby (shafts 1 and 3), pass the tabby shuttle; treadle pattern block A again, pass the pattern shuttle; treadle the other tabby (shafts 2 and 4), pass the tabby shuttle. Repeat the pattern passes as many times as desired to build the block height: 4 pattern passes produces a square block at most setts. Swap to pattern block B (shafts 3 and 4) to shift the block position. The pattern weft should be significantly heavier than the tabby weft to make the floats visible against the ground.

Remove the cloth from the loom and secure the warp ends with overhand knots or hemstitching woven in before removal. Hand wash in warm water and press under a damp cloth while slightly damp. Monk's belt cloth relaxes noticeably on washing and the floats become more prominent as the tabby shrinks slightly around them.

items.problem: The pattern floats are not visible and the cloth looks like plain weave
items.cause: The pattern weft is too similar in weight and colour to the tabby weft
items.fix: Use a pattern weft at least twice as heavy as the tabby weft, or wind two ends of the pattern colour together in the shuttle. The contrast in weight makes the floats sit proud of the ground cloth.

items.problem: The blocks shift position when they should stay aligned
items.cause: The wrong treadle was used for one of the tabby rows, breaking the tabby sequence
items.fix: Always alternate tabby treadles strictly: left, right, left, right. Each pattern pass must be followed by the correct alternating tabby treadle; stepping two consecutive tabby treadles from the same side breaks the plain weave.
